title: Agent .md Profile → Self-Contained Agent Loop: Feasibility Case Study
request: Turn each agent .md profile into a proper self-contained agent loop with its own lifecycle for consistency
executive_summary: The proposal is feasible but represents a multi-phase architectural project, not a quick refactor. The current architecture has strong foundations (Effect service layer, structured config loading, InstanceState pattern, permission system) but the agent loop itself is a single generic streaming loop that varies only by prompt + permissions. The entire wave-based orchestration model — cartographer → architect → critic → gate → executor → validator → stress → repair — is a prompt-driven convention with zero structural enforcement. Making it per-agent requires a planned extraction. Estimated effort: 1-2 weeks for lifecycle decorator (Approach A), 3-4 weeks for phase-enforced runners (Approach B).

current_architecture.agents_in_system: ~51 .md files in ~/.config/opencode/agents/ + 8 built-in agents in agent.ts = ~59 total agent types
current_architecture.loading_system: packages/opencode/src/config/agent.ts — globs {agent,agents}/**/*.md, parses YAML frontmatter, normalizes into Agent.Info
current_architecture.registry: packages/opencode/src/agent/agent.ts — Effect Context Service with InstanceState<Record<string, Info>>, built once per project
current_architecture.processor: packages/opencode/src/session/processor.ts — single generic LLM streaming loop for ALL agents
current_architecture.task_spawning: packages/opencode/src/tool/task.ts — creates child sessions with derived permissions, uses same processor
current_architecture.key_insight: The entire wave lifecycle (cartographer → architect → critic → gate → executor → validator → stress → repair) has ZERO runtime enforcement — it's a prompt-only convention

structurally_enforced: Agent schema validation — .md frontmatter validated against Schema.Struct
Permission ruleset merging — 4-layer merge with subagent session derivation
Task tool restriction — agent must explicitly allow task: to spawn sub-subagents
Background flag gating — OPENCODE_EXPERIMENTAL_BACKGROUND_SUBAGENTS runtime flag required
Editor hiding — hidden:true agents filtered from @ autocomplete
prompt_driven_only: Wave sequence ordering (cartographer before architect before executor)
Parallel fan-out of sub-subagents (serial vs parallel is a prompt suggestion)
Verification standards ('find 5 examples', 'every claim must cite file:line')
Handoff JSON formats (executor handoff, lane-owner handoff — no unified schema)
Repair cycle limits ('max 3 cycles then escalate' — no structural limit)
'Never ask user' rule — prompt instruction, not structural enforcer

sub_subagent_ambiguity.finding: Many sub-subagents described in agent .md profiles (surface-mapper, module-grapher, root-cause-analyst, impact-assessor, etc.) are referenced as if they're dedicated agent types, but they may not have dedicated .md files in ~/.config/opencode/agents/. The system relies on task(subagent_type) finding an existing agent by name — if no .md file exists, it either reuses other agent types or the task tool falls back.
sub_subagent_ambiguity.impact: The subagent fan-out tables in .md profiles are aspirational rather than actual — they describe ideal subagent types that may exist only as prompt-notional roles, not as runnable agent configurations.

adversary_consolidation_opportunity.finding: The 8 adversary profiles (claim-adversary, evidence-adversary, authority-adversary, security-adversary, recovery-adversary, production-proof-adversary, lane-collision-adversary, publication-truth-adversary) follow an identical template with negligible variation — same persona ('patient, well-articulated senior colleague'), same output format (JSON repair directive), same article sections. They differ only in the specific attack domain.
adversary_consolidation_opportunity.impact: This is an 8x code consolidation opportunity. A single parameterized adversary generator could produce all 8 variants from a domain parameter, dramatically reducing lifecycle duplication.

handoff_fragmentation.finding: Every agent type has a different handoff format defined only in prose: executor uses JSON with verification block, critic uses Markdown tables with 7-axis scores, architect uses JSON with fixes/risks/validation, lane-owner uses send_message(body=JSON.stringify({...})). No unified handoff schema exists across all agents.
handoff_fragmentation.impact: verify_handoff tool validates a generic contract but cannot check field completeness or structure for specific agent types.

approach_a.name: Lifecycle Decorator
approach_a.description: Add optional lifecycle: field to AgentSchema. Create LifecycleEngine service that wraps existing processor with phase-awareness. Advisory-only (warning-level) validation. Backward compatible.

approach_b.name: Phase-Enforced Agent Runners
approach_b.description: Discriminated union of agent types with type-specific runners. Each runner enforces phase ordering, parallel fan-out, and phase-gated tool access. Generic loop remains fallback for simple agents.
approach_b.recommended_as: Medium-term after telemetry from Approach A

approach_c.name: Full Workflow Engine
approach_c.description: DAG-based workflow engine with lifecycle definitions as JSON schema, phase enforcement, conditional branching, native repair cycles, escalation paths.
approach_c.feasibility: LOW — too ambitious for current stage

code_seams_for_lifecycle.config/agent.ts:33-76: Add optional lifecycle: field to AgentSchema
code_seams_for_lifecycle.agent/agent.ts:29-50: Add lifecycle field to Agent.Info runtime type
code_seams_for_lifecycle.agent/agent.ts:82-327: InstanceState.make() is the natural lifecycle state initialization hook
code_seams_for_lifecycle.session/processor.ts:87-863: Wrap session processor with LifecycleEngine that selects behavior by agent type
code_seams_for_lifecycle.tool/task.ts:103-286: Propagate lifecycle context when spawning subagent sessions
code_seams_for_lifecycle.permission/index.ts: Add phase-aware tool gating via additional ruleset in merge chain

recommendation: Adopt Approach A first (lifecycle decorator, 1-2 weeks) to get lifecycle telemetry and schema. Then based on telemetry data, implement Approach B for the highest-value agent types: cartographer (parallel fan-out), executor (edit-verify cycle), lane-owner (full wave lifecycle). Defer Approach C until after Postgres migration. The InstanceState pattern already provides the lifecycle state seam — no new infrastructure needed.

key_findings.finding: Agent lifecycle is entirely prompt-driven — no runtime enforcement of wave sequences, phase ordering, parallel fan-out, or verification standards
No structural enforcement of phase ordering — tools are gated by permission set at session creation, not dynamically by lifecycle phase
Sub-subagents referenced in .md profiles (surface-mapper, module-grapher, etc.) may not have dedicated .md files — the fan-out tables are aspirational
8 adversary profiles share ~95% identical template — strong consolidation opportunity into parameterized generator
Handoff formats are fragmented across agent types with no unified schema — verify_handoff can't validate per-type completeness
InstanceState pattern is the natural lifecycle seam and requires no new infrastructure
Permission system is mature enough to support phase-aware tool gating via additional ruleset in merge chain
